summary: Big day for the SE BeltLine corridor with Reynoldstown finally breaking ground on that long-stalled corner project and a new food hall opening next month near East Lake Golf Club. Meanwhile, the airport situation is still a mess with ICE agents basically standing around while TSA lines stretch for hours.

topStory.headline: Reynoldstown Corner Project Near BeltLine Finally Breaks Ground
topStory.summary: After nearly five years of sitting as a cleared lot, that corner where Chester Avenue meets Fulton Terrace is finally seeing some action. Embry Development took over from Toll Brothers and is moving forward with 85 residential units just a block from the Eastside Trail. You know that spot if you've walked the BeltLine near Memorial Drive.
topStory.body:
Remember that cleared corner lot in Reynoldstown that's been taunting BeltLine walkers for years? Well, groundwork has finally started at Chester Avenue and Fulton Terrace, about a block north of Memorial Drive. Atlanta-based Embry Development Company picked up the 2-acre site from national developer Toll Brothers two years ago and is now moving forward with 85 residential units.

The project will include both townhomes and for-sale condos, all within easy walking distance of the ever-popular Eastside Trail. According to Embry Development managing partner, this represents a significant investment in the neighborhood's continued transformation along the BeltLine corridor.

This development has been a long time coming. The site was originally cleared nearly five years ago, leaving neighbors wondering when something would actually get built. Now that shovels are in the ground, it's another sign of the continued growth and investment happening throughout the SE BeltLine neighborhoods.
topStory.imageAlt: Construction equipment and groundwork at the Reynoldstown development site near the BeltLine

stories.imageAlt: Construction equipment and groundwork at the Reynoldstown development site near the BeltLine

stories.headline: Decatur's Missing Middle Housing Village Nears Opening
stories.summary: Cottages at Midway is bringing 18 standalone houses to unincorporated Decatur, designed as a pocket neighborhood with shared courtyards and green space. The goal is creating more accessible single-family housing near downtown Decatur, where median home values have climbed above $700,000. A soft launch starts next week.
stories.body:
Finally, some relief for ITP home buyers who've been priced out of Decatur proper. Cottages at Midway is putting the finishing touches on 18 standalone houses at 2883 and 2893 Midway Road, designed as a village-style community with shared courtyards and greenspace.

This is exactly the kind of "missing middle" housing that planners talk about but rarely see built. The pocket neighborhood replaces two underused lots totaling 2.1 acres on a residential street, creating more accessible single-family options in an area where median home values have climbed to more than $700,000.

The developers are planning a soft launch next week to drum up interest, and honestly, it's about time. Decatur's popularity has made it nearly impossible for regular folks to afford, so creative solutions like this village concept could be a model for other intown neighborhoods struggling with affordability.
stories.imageAlt: Site map showing the layout of Cottages at Midway village-style housing development

stories.headline: West End BeltLine Townhomes Start Taking Shape
stories.summary: GW Parkside (formerly Hopkins and White Townhomes) is bringing 23 for-sale townhomes to the West End along the BeltLine. Ackerman & Co., the same developer behind Lee + White, started demolition a year ago and now has the first seven units nearing completion. They're targeting spring for the first sales.
stories.body:
That infill project along the BeltLine in the West End is finally starting to look like something. GW Parkside, the townhome development at 600 Hopkins Street, has been renamed and is showing real progress with the first seven units nearing top-out.

Ackerman & Co., the same folks behind Lee + White and its expanding food and office scene, started this project a year ago with demolition. Now they're bringing 23 for-sale townhomes to this stretch of the BeltLine, with the first units expected to hit the market this spring.

This is relatively rare for the area since most BeltLine development has been rental apartments. Having for-sale townhomes gives people a chance to actually own something along the trail, assuming they can afford it. The location puts residents within walking distance of Lee + White's restaurants and just a short bike ride to downtown.
stories.imageAlt: Rendering of GW Parkside townhomes under construction along the BeltLine in West End

stories.headline: Robert Montwaid's East Side Food Hall Opens Next Month
stories.summary: The man behind NYC's Gansevoort Market is bringing his third Atlanta food concept to Vela Park near East Lake Golf Club. Vela Park Food Works will feature six chef-driven concepts plus craft cocktails and outdoor dining, with a mid-April opening planned. This marks another major food destination for the eastside.
stories.body:
Get ready for another food hall, but this one has serious pedigree behind it. Robert Montwaid, who created New York City's famous Gansevoort Market, is opening Vela Park Food Works in mid-April at the new rental complex near East Lake Golf Club.

This will be Montwaid's third food concept in Atlanta, and he's promising six different chef-driven concepts along with craft cocktails and roomy outdoor dining. The food hall serves as the retail centerpiece of the nearly 12-acre Vela Park community, giving eastside residents another major dining destination.

With Krog Street Market, Ponce City Market, and now this, Atlanta's food hall game is getting seriously competitive. The east side has been hungry for more dining options beyond the usual suspects, so this could fill a real gap for folks in the Kirkwood, Edgewood, and East Atlanta areas who don't want to trek to O4W every time they want elevated food options.
stories.imageAlt: Interior rendering of Vela Park Food Works showing dining areas and food stalls

stories.headline: Mushi Ni Brings Asian-American Comfort Food to Inman Park
stories.summary: Chefs Michael Le and Tanya Jimenez opened their pop-up-turned-restaurant on Elizabeth Street today, just off the Freedom Park Trail. Expect soft shell crab bao, Peking chicken platters, and Thai tea martinis for lunch and dinner. Both chefs have serious restaurant backgrounds, including Jimenez's time with Gordon Ramsay in NYC.
stories.body:
Inman Park just got a serious upgrade in the Asian food department. Mushi Ni officially opened today on Elizabeth Street, right off the Freedom Park Trail, bringing Asian-American comfort food from chefs Michael Le and Tanya Jimenez.

These aren't amateur hour cooks. Jimenez worked for Gordon Ramsay in New York before moving to Atlanta, and both have put in serious time in restaurant kitchens. Their menu hits all the comfort food notes with items like soft shell crab bao, chicken longanisa bao, umami fries, crab Rangoon puffs, and Peking chicken platters that feed two for $25.

The location is perfect for grabbing food before or after walking the Freedom Park Trail, and the fact that they've graduated from pop-up status shows there's real demand for this kind of elevated Asian comfort food. Inman Park's restaurant scene has been dominated by the same few spots for years, so having something fresh and different is exactly what the neighborhood needed.
stories.imageAlt: Peking chicken platter at Mushi Ni restaurant showing Asian-American comfort food

stories.headline: Lewis Barbecue Brings Austin-Style BBQ to the BeltLine
stories.summary: The smokehouse at Ansley Mall behind The Cook's Warehouse features six 22-foot smokers running 24/7 in a panoramic loft overlooking the BeltLine bridge. Pitmaster John Lewis, who helped weld the smokers from 1,000-gallon propane tanks, is turning out some of Atlanta's best brisket with a factory-like rhythm.
stories.body:
Lewis Barbecue just opened at Ansley Mall and it might have the most impressive smokehouse setup in the city. Six 22-foot smokers fill a spacious loft with panoramic windows overlooking the new pedestrian bridge to the BeltLine, running 24 hours a day in staggered eight-hour shifts.

Pitmaster John Lewis helped weld these smokers out of 1,000-gallon propane tanks, and the factory rhythm is producing some of the best brisket you'll find in Atlanta. The location behind The Cook's Warehouse puts it right in the path of BeltLine foot traffic, which should help introduce Atlanta to this Austin-by-way-of-Charleston style.

The timing couldn't be better with the BeltLine connection and all the foot traffic from the Ansley Mall development. Having serious barbecue this close to the trail gives people another reason to walk or bike the BeltLine, and honestly, we needed more quality barbecue options in this part of town.
stories.imageAlt: Barbecue platter from Lewis Barbecue showing smoked meats and sides

stories.headline: Decatur's Complete Streets Makeover Nearly Finished
stories.summary: The West Howard Avenue project between downtown Decatur and East Lake MARTA is wrapping up after nearly a decade of planning. The roughly one-mile stretch now features a two-way cycle track with concrete barriers, new sidewalks, and traffic calming measures to transform the former drag strip into a people-friendly corridor.
stories.body:
After nearly a decade of planning, Decatur's transformation of West Howard Avenue from a high-speed car corridor into a Complete Street is almost done. The roughly one-mile project between Commerce Drive and Paden Circle now features a two-way cycle track with concrete barriers, upgraded sidewalks, and traffic calming measures.

This stretch used to be a notorious drag strip for motorists racing between downtown Decatur and the East Lake MARTA station. Now it actually looks like a street designed for humans, not just cars. The concrete-barrier-protected bike lane is a particularly nice touch since it gives cyclists real protection from traffic.

The project connects the southern edges of downtown Decatur to Atlanta's city limits and the East Lake MARTA station, making it easier for people to bike or walk between the two areas. It's the kind of infrastructure investment that should make both Decatur residents and eastside Atlanta folks happy, assuming drivers adjust to the new reality of actually sharing the road.
stories.imageAlt: New two-way cycle track on West Howard Avenue in Decatur with concrete barriers

stories.headline: City Council Advances ICE Controls, Questions Fire Union Contract
stories.summary: The Public Safety Committee unanimously passed resolutions barring ICE detention facilities in city limits and requiring documentation when ICE agents are present during police operations. The full council votes April 20. Dozens also showed up criticizing Mayor Dickens for not signing the firefighters' first-ever union contract.
stories.body:
City Council's Public Safety Committee had a busy day, unanimously advancing two resolutions aimed at controlling ICE activities in Atlanta. One bars Immigration and Customs Enforcement detention facilities within city limits, while the other requires Atlanta police to document when ICE agents are present during their operations.

The timing is notable given ICE agents are currently deployed at Hartsfield-Jackson to help with the airport security mess. The full council will vote on both resolutions April 20, and given the unanimous committee support, they'll likely pass.

But the bigger fireworks came from dozens of residents who showed up to blast Mayor Dickens for refusing to sign the city's first-ever contract with the Atlanta firefighters' union. The council ratified the contract last April, but Dickens has been sitting on it ever since. Firefighters and their supporters made it clear they're not happy about the mayor's foot-dragging on what should have been a historic moment for labor relations in the city.
stories.imageAlt: Atlanta City Council Public Safety Committee meeting with residents in attendance

stories.headline: ICE Agents Deploy to Atlanta Airport But Travelers Say They're Not Helping
stories.summary: Federal immigration agents are walking around Hartsfield-Jackson to assist with security as TSA staffing shortages create hourslong lines. But travelers report the agents are mostly standing around and not actually helping move lines. The deployment comes as more than 40% of TSA agents have been calling out during the partial shutdown.
stories.body:
ICE agents are now patrolling Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International Airport, but if you're stuck in those brutal TSA lines, don't expect much help. Travelers report seeing the armed federal agents in tactical gear but say they're mostly just standing around, not actually helping with security screenings or line management.

The deployment comes as TSA faces massive staffing shortages, with more than 40% of Atlanta TSA workers reportedly calling out during the ongoing partial government shutdown. Wait times hit four hours on some days, prompting airport officials to bring in whatever federal help they could get.

Mayor Dickens assured residents Sunday that the ICE agents won't engage in immigration enforcement during this deployment, but travelers are questioning whether the extra bodies are making any real difference. One traveler told reporters the agents are "not doing anything" while security lines stretch for hours. It's a visible reminder of how the federal shutdown is creating chaos at the world's busiest airport.
stories.imageAlt: ICE agents in tactical gear at Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International Airport

stories.headline: SW Atlanta Residents Block Alcohol Sales at Controversial Gas Station
stories.summary: Faye Floyd and other NPU P residents scored a victory when the developer for 3397 Greenbriar Parkway SW withdrew their alcohol license application. The community has been fighting the gas station project since last year, arguing it violates the city's own law banning new gas stations in their neighborhood.
stories.body:
Southwest Atlanta residents just won a battle in their ongoing war against a controversial gas station project. Faye Floyd, who chairs Neighborhood Planning Unit P, got word hours before a key zoning meeting that the developer for 3397 Greenbriar Parkway SW had withdrawn their application for an alcohol license.

This represents a significant victory for community members who've been fighting this project since last year. They argue the gas station violates Atlanta's own law that bans new gas stations from being built in their neighborhood, but city officials have been pushing the project forward anyway.

While Floyd acknowledges they didn't stop the gas station entirely, preventing alcohol sales is still a major win. Gas stations with alcohol licenses tend to create more problems for surrounding neighborhoods, so blocking that component shows community organizing can still make a difference when residents stay engaged and hold the city accountable to its own rules.
stories.imageAlt: Community meeting with southwest Atlanta residents discussing gas station opposition
